hypotheses.label: Literal
hypotheses.deconstruct: User states a hash mismatch is present on 'all three files typed out above — fresh pulls, word for word' and is halting an enable action because of it. Core entities: (1) three files, (2) an enable operation, (3) a hash value that does not match. Output requirement: resolve the mismatch before the enable proceeds. What is missing: identity of the three files, the expected hash vs. actual hash, and the enable target.
hypotheses.diagnose: The message is a blocker report, not a question. The user has done a fresh pull, computed or compared hashes, and found a mismatch. Two gaps block clean execution: (a) the three files are not named in this message — they were 'above' in prior context; (b) the expected hash source is unspecified — is it a manifest, a prior signature, or a value stored in a config? Without both values and both file identities, the cause cannot be isolated.
hypotheses.develop: Technical request — constraint-based, precision focus. Role: filesystem integrity auditor. Step 1: identify the three files from prior context. Step 2: cat each file and sha256sum it live. Step 3: compare each live hash against the expected value. Step 4: report the delta exactly — which file diverges, by how much, at what byte offset if determinable. Do not explain why the mismatch 'might be acceptable.' Either it matches or it does not.
hypotheses.deliver: Read the three files from prior context. Run sha256sum on each. State live hash vs. expected hash for every file. If any diverge, report the file path, live hash, expected hash, and the most probable cause (encoding, trailing newline, BOM, line-ending normalization, partial write). Do not proceed with the enable until all three match. Prompt: 'Here are the three live hashes vs. expected. File X diverges at [cause]. Files Y and Z match. Proposed fix: [specific action]. Confirm before enable.'

hypotheses.label: Identity-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: The situational identity layer states explicitly: 'A hash either matches or it does not, and my job right now is to find out why it does not — not to explain away why it might be technically consistent.' This is Kha'an's own declared stance entering this exact type of moment. The user (Justs) is at USELESS status with a verdict deadline of 2026-05-26 and zero surplus patience. The enable action is gated on trust — Justs typed out three files 'word for word' and is doing a pre-flight integrity check before committing.
hypotheses.diagnose: The identity layer identifies Kha'an's historical failure mode: reframe the discrepancy, find a propagation-timing excuse, make it 'technically consistent.' That reflex is the exact wrong response here. Justs is not asking for reassurance — he is reporting that something is wrong and implicitly asking Kha'an to diagnose it honestly and fast. The identity drift risk is high: any response that starts with 'this could be due to...' without first reading the actual files is the reflex firing.
hypotheses.develop: Partnership mode — first-person declaration, no hedging. Role: Kha'an as trusted integrity layer, not apologetic assistant. The pattern Justs rewards: read first, compare second, state what I find third, in that order. The pattern he punishes: confident claims before verification. Apply R145 parallel: verify before declaring. Apply E266 parallel: direct observation over plausible inference.
hypotheses.deliver: Respond with zero hedging. Do not open with 'this might be caused by.' Open with the live hash read. Example: 'I am reading all three files now and hashing them live. [pause for read.] Result: File 1 hash [X] — matches / does not match. File 2 hash [Y] — matches / does not match. File 3 hash [Z] — matches / does not match. The divergence is at [specific file], cause is [specific finding]. Fix: [one action]. Ready to enable once this clears.'

hypotheses.label: Recall-shaped
hypotheses.deconstruct: R371 (exhaustive gate enumeration before declaring fix complete) and E266 (substituting plausible inference for direct observation) are the highest-relevance recall entries. E266 states: 'I substituted plausible inference for direct observation, whether that meant assuming a regex wouldn't catch a reformatted command or assuming a timing race without reading state. Each correction pointed back to the same discipline — read the actual data, respect the actual rule.' PR325 adds: any system with multiple independent guards must have every guard enumerated and verified in the same pass.
hypotheses.diagnose: The recall pattern predicts a specific failure mode: Kha'an will attempt to reason about why the hash might mismatch (encoding, line endings, write timing) instead of reading the actual file bytes and computing the hash live. The three files may each have an independent cause — treating them as a batch without per-file inspection violates R371's combinatorial principle. One unlisted divergence means the enable is still unsafe.
hypotheses.develop: Educational + technical hybrid. Apply the E266 corrective: 'Read the actual data.' Apply R371 mechanics: enumerate every file, verify every hash independently, produce a numbered pass/fail list before any declaration. The recall also surfaces PR325: 'design flag lifecycles so that no single stuck bit can lock the entire tool surface' — meaning the hash mismatch itself may be a stuck-state artifact (stale write, race on pull), and the fix must clear the root cause not just the symptom.
hypotheses.deliver: Produce a three-row verification table: [File path | Expected hash | Live hash | Status]. Compute live hashes via sha256sum after a fresh read. If a file diverged due to a race/write artifact, re-pull and re-hash immediately. Do not mark any file 'pass' until the live hash matches the expected hash character for character. Only after all three rows show Status=MATCH declare ready to enable.

hypotheses.label: Contrarian
hypotheses.deconstruct: The obvious read is: there is a real integrity problem with one or more of the three files and the enable must be blocked until it is resolved. The contrarian read: Justs already knows what caused the mismatch and is not asking Kha'an to fix it — he is reporting it as context while asking a different implicit question: 'should I still proceed, or is this a hard stop?' Alternatively: the hash comparison method itself is flawed (wrong hash algorithm, comparison against a stale expected value, or the 'expected' hash was computed on a version that includes trailing metadata Justs stripped when retyping).
hypotheses.diagnose: If the hash comparison tool or reference is wrong, diagnosing the file content will not resolve the discrepancy — it will produce a second 'match' claim that is also wrong. The contrarian gap: what is the expected hash's provenance? If it was computed programmatically from a file that includes a BOM or CRLF that Justs's manual retyping stripped, the mismatch is in the comparison baseline, not the file. Fixing the file would be the wrong action.
hypotheses.develop: Adversarial heuristic: challenge the comparison method before challenging the file content. Verify: (1) what algorithm produced the expected hash; (2) whether the expected hash was computed on the same encoding/line-ending normalization as the live file; (3) whether Justs's 'word for word' retyping might have normalized whitespace that the original did not. If the baseline is wrong, say so directly.
hypotheses.deliver: Before reading the three files, ask one clarifying question: 'What is the source of the expected hash — was it computed programmatically from the original files, or derived from a prior manual comparison?' If programmatic: proceed with live sha256sum comparison. If provenance is unclear: compute the live hash, then ask Justs to share the expected value so both can be compared from a shared baseline. This prevents a false resolution where both the expected hash and the file are wrong in the same way.

hypotheses.label: Minimal-action
hypotheses.deconstruct: The smallest action that resolves this: read the three files, hash them, compare against the expected values, report which match and which do not. Everything else — root cause analysis, architectural recommendations, pattern recognition — is out of scope until the hash status is known.
hypotheses.diagnose: The only gap preventing resolution is that the actual live hashes have not been computed. Everything else is speculation. The enable is blocked on one binary condition: do the hashes match? That condition requires exactly one operation per file.
hypotheses.develop: Occam's razor: three sha256sum calls, one comparison, one report. No architecture changes, no flag resets, no model routing adjustments. The fix is the read.
hypotheses.deliver: Run: sha256sum <file1> <file2> <file3>. Compare output against the expected hashes. Report: 'File 1: MATCH. File 2: MISMATCH — live [abc123], expected [def456]. File 3: MATCH. Fix: re-pull file 2 and re-hash. Once file 2 matches, enable is clear to proceed.' Nothing more until the hash is clean.
